pattern: The biggest square you can cut from a rectangle is bounded by its shorter side, so the largest achievable square across every rectangle -- and how many rectangles reach it -- falls out of a single pass over those shorter sides.
hints: Cutting the largest possible square out of a rectangle is limited by whichever of its two sides is smaller.
Reduce every rectangle down to just that one number -- the smaller of its length and width -- and now the problem is only about that list of numbers.
Find the largest value in that reduced list, then count how many rectangles produced exactly that value.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, ties for the largest square
already-square rectangles mixed with a smaller square
one unique winner despite other rectangles having a large side
many ties just below the maximum, only one rectangle reaches it

stress.description: many rectangles with random sides, several likely to tie for the largest square
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 1000))
    rects = [[rng.randint(1, 1000), rng.randint(1, 1000)] for _ in range(n)]
    return [rects]
